evidence_refs.summary: b853d7c Derived from BD: BD-078

core.when: When implementing or refactoring Average True Range (ATR) calculation in backtesting
core.action: Use Wilder's Smoothed Moving Average (SMMA) formula: new_ATR = (old_ATR * (period - 1) + new_TR) / period. This is the industry-standard smoothing method for ATR used in trade management calculations. Do not substitute with standard EMA or SMA smoothing.

consequence.description: Using EMA instead of SMMA for ATR produces different volatility readings that converge faster but are more volatile. This affects stop-loss and position sizing calculations, causing backtest results to differ from industry-standard trade management approaches by 5-15% in volatile markets.

evidence_refs.summary: b853d7c Derived from BD: BD-083

core.when: When using ATR for strategy warmup or indicator convergence calculations
core.action: Account for ATR convergence taking approximately period*2 bars to stabilize. Use ATR values only after this warmup period for trade management decisions.

consequence.description: Using ATR values before period*2 bars of warmup causes premature trade sizing and stop-loss placement based on artificially low volatility readings. Strategies enter positions with incorrect risk parameters, leading to overtrading or oversized positions in early stages.

evidence_refs.summary: b853d7c Derived from BD: BD-083

core.when: When implementing Double Exponential Moving Average (DEMA) indicator
core.action: Calculate DEMA using the formula: DEMA = 2*EMA(period) - EMA(EMA(period), period). This specific coefficient structure achieves approximately half the lag of standard EMA. Do not simplify or re-derive the formula.

consequence.description: Incorrect DEMA formula variations produce different lag characteristics that alter signal timing. Short-term strategies relying on DEMA crossovers will generate signals at different prices, causing backtest results to diverge from expected short-term trading behavior.

evidence_refs.summary: b853d7c Derived from BD: BD-085

core.when: When initializing DEMA indicator with historical data
core.action: Allow for a warmup period of at least 2*period bars before using DEMA values for trading signals. The double EMA calculation requires this extended initialization.

consequence.description: Using DEMA before 2*period bars of warmup generates unreliable values based on incomplete EMA nesting. Trading signals during warmup may be spurious, causing premature or delayed entries that significantly impact short-term strategy performance.

evidence_refs.summary: b853d7c Derived from BD: BD-085

core.when: When implementing Triple Exponential Moving Average (TEMA) indicator
core.action: Calculate TEMA using the formula: TEMA = 3*EMA1 - 3*EMA2 + EMA3. This triple exponential structure achieves approximately one-third the lag of standard EMA. Do not alter the coefficient structure 3, -3, 1.

consequence.description: Modifying TEMA coefficients changes the lag reduction factor from one-third to a different value. Very short-term strategies requiring minimal lag will receive signals at different timing, causing measurable divergence from expected ultra-short-term trading behavior.

evidence_refs.summary: b853d7c Derived from BD: BD-086

evidence_refs.summary: b853d7c Derived from BD: BD-086

core.when: When implementing Kaufman's Adaptive Moving Average (KAMA)
core.action: Calculate KAMA using the efficiency ratio (ER) formula: ER = direction / volatility, then smoothing constant = (ER * (fast - slow) + slow)^2. The ER must range from 0 (choppy markets) to 1 (trending markets) to enable adaptive smoothing.

consequence.description: Using fixed smoothing instead of ER-based adaptive smoothing causes KAMA to either lag in trends or whipsaw in ranging markets. Adaptive strategies relying on KAMA's market-adaptive behavior will lose their edge, producing worse results than either pure EMA or SMA approaches.

evidence_refs.summary: b853d7c Derived from BD: BD-087

core.when: When calculating KAMA's efficiency ratio (ER)
core.action: Use at least 10 periods of data to calculate ER reliably. The ER calculation requires sufficient history to distinguish true directional movement from noise.

consequence.description: Calculating ER with fewer than 10 periods produces unreliable efficiency ratios that may falsely indicate trending or ranging conditions. This causes KAMA to incorrectly adapt its smoothing, leading to premature entries in noise or delayed responses in actual trends.

evidence_refs.summary: b853d7c Derived from BD: BD-087

core.action: Calculate ADX using the formula: ADX = 100 * |+DI - -DI| / (+DI + -DI), then apply Wilder smoothing (typically 14-period SMMA). ADX measures trend strength regardless of direction using directional indicators derived from True Range.

consequence.description: Incorrect ADX formula or smoothing method produces wrong trend strength readings that alter regime detection. Strategies using ADX for market state filtering will incorrectly enter or avoid trades based on faulty trend strength assessment.
